VO_EN: You wake up at six fifteen.
The alarm doesn't really wake you.
You were already half awake, staring at the ceiling.
You drive to work in a car that's almost paid off.
You kiss your daughter on the forehead before you leave.
She barely looks up from her cereal.
The doctor sits across from you in a small room with fluorescent lights.
There's a model kidney on his desk.
He says two words you don't understand at first.
They've been dying for a while.
And once they stop, so do you.
You go to the transplant office on a Tuesday.
The waiting room has plastic chairs and a television nobody watches.
You fill out twelve pages of forms.
The woman behind the desk doesn't look up.
She's done this a thousand times.
You fold the paper and put it in your wallet.
You don't know why you keep it there.
Every Tuesday you sit in a chair for four hours.
A machine cleans your blood because your body can't.
The needle goes into a fistula in your arm.
That's the part that bothers you.
The nurse checks the tubes without speaking.
Her shoes squeak on the floor.
That sound stays with you longer than anything the doctor said.
The man in the chair next to you falls asleep every session.
One Tuesday his chair is empty.
Your number moves from eight forty-seven to eight forty-three.
That means four people ahead of you died.
That is how the list works.
You sleep twelve hours and wake up tired.
Your skin itches in places you can't reach.
Your daughter asks why you nap so much.
You tell her you're just a little under the weather.
You've said that for five months now.
You scratch the inside of your wrist without thinking.
The skin there is dry from the tape they use at dialysis.
Your wife smiles when you catch her looking.
But you've seen the face she makes when she thinks you can't see.
She's been looking at life insurance pages on her laptop.
She closes them when you walk in.
Someone at the dialysis center leans over one afternoon.
He's been watching you for weeks.
You've noticed but you haven't said anything.
He says he knows a guy.
You think about it in the shower that night.
A hotel lobby three weeks later.
The carpet smells like cleaning product.
The man wears a dark jacket.
He doesn't give you his name.
He says he can get you a kidney.
He says it the way someone sells a used car.
The coffee on the table between you goes cold.
He says you don't need to know the details.
You say you'll wait your turn.
He slides a phone number across the table.
Written on the back of a receipt.
You put it in your wallet next to the waiting list number.
You don't look at either of them on the drive home.
Three weeks later you're in the emergency room.
Your wife drove you there at two in the morning.
Your wife is in the hallway.
She's on the phone with someone.
Her voice is steady but her hands aren't.
The IV drip beside you clicks every few seconds.
You lie in bed that night.
Your daughter's birthday is in seven months.
You've been doing the math every night.
You try to picture her opening presents without you.
You turn over and face the wall.
You can hear your wife breathing in the next room.
You find the number in your wallet.
It's been there for three weeks.
The receipt is soft from being folded too many times.
You sit in the car in the driveway.
The windshield is fogging up from your breath.
He answers on the second ring.
He says: I knew you'd call back.
He asks if you can travel.
He asks if you have the money.
You've already thought about where it comes from.
He says he'll call with a date and a city.
You don't ask where the kidney comes from.
You hang up and sit there for a while.
The parking lot is half empty.
A streetlight buzzes somewhere above you.
You don't remember starting the engine.
A city you've never been to.
The air is warm and heavy.
The taxi driver asks if you're here for business.
You told your wife you're at a medical conference.
You don't know which is worse.
A clinic with no name on the door.
Staff walk past you without looking up.
Someone else hands you a gown.
You hear something from the other room.
Your fingers grip the edge of the table.
For half a second they don't grip.
There's something inside you that wasn't there yesterday.
A nurse hands you a form the next morning.
You know every word on this page is a lie.
The flight home is seven hours.
You sit by the window and don't read, don't sleep, don't think about anything you can name.
Your reflection in the glass looks the same.
Your daughter runs to the door.
The color is already coming back to your face.
Your wife holds you and doesn't let go for a long time.
She doesn't ask about the trip.
Your eyes don't match your mouth.
Every morning you open a bottle.
Your body will try to reject what's inside you.
You'll take it every day for the rest of your life.
You keep the bottle in the medicine cabinet behind the mouthwash.
Every pill is a clock that resets.
A friend asks at dinner one night.
You've rehearsed this so many times it almost sounds true.
You take a sip of water.
You see a headline one morning while making breakfast.
Everything blurs for a second - the kitchen, the light, your daughter's voice from the other room - and then it comes back.
The phone rings one evening while you're doing the dishes.
They ask if you need anything.
You put the phone face down on the counter and go back to the dishes.
You wake up at six fifteen.
You kiss your daughter on the forehead.
She still barely looks up from her cereal.
But inside you there's something that will never be yours.
And you will never ask who it belonged to.
If you've ever wondered what it feels like to carry someone else inside you.
And never ask how they got there.
